title: "podman build" is slow, even for simple single line containerfile takes 1 minute on M2 macos 14.3.1 Sonoma
body:
### Issue Description

Since about the time of having updated to macOS 14 Sonoma on my M2 while keeping the same old podman version, was something half a year older than current 4.9.2, even simple one-liner containerfiles take more than a minute to build an image. Also with all data downloaded to the local cache.

Example, when running the following, each repeated run only completes after about 1 minute, same results on 4.9.1 and 4.9.2:
```
% podman -v
podman version 4.9.2

% qemu-system-aarch64 --version
QEMU emulator version 8.2.1

% echo FROM centos:stream > centostest.txt

Repeat this to have the cache filled:
% time podman build -t testimg -f centostest.txt
podman build -t testimg -f centostest.txt  48.42s user 29.57s system 110% cpu 1:10.54 total
```

During that minute, most of the time, the qemu-system-aarch64 process runs at about 150% CPU utilization and podman-remote at about 130%.

On an M1 Mac with the same OS and podman versions, it's quick as normal, completing within seconds. Before the macOS upgrade, it was also fast on this M2 Mac.

When adding --log-level=debug, it visible how it hangs at this step:
DEBU[0000] DoRequest Method: POST URI: http://d/v4.9.1/libpod/build 

Then after the minute, it continues with:
STEP 1/1: FROM centos:stream
COMMIT testimg

Maybe related to #19155 ?


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
echo FROM centos:stream > centostest.txt
time podman build -t testimg -f centostest.txt


### Describe the results you received

podman build always takes about 1 minute, even with files in cache

### Describe the results you expected

podman build completes within a few seconds

### podman info output


comments.body:
When starting it with podman machine start --log-level=debug 
the connection fails:

Error: Post "http://d/v4.9.2/libpod/build?dockerfile=%5B%22centostest.txt%22%...story=0&output=testimg&outputformat=application%2Fvnd.oci.image.manifest.v1%2Bjson&pullpolicy=missing&rm=1&shmsize=67108864&t=testimg": io: read/write on closed pipe
I installed podman and qemu (automatic dependency) on both Macs with brew install podman.
Could you try with the apple hypervisor?
This sounds like you are using a big context directory, try putting the containerfile in an otherwise empty directory.

> This sounds like you are using a big context directory, try putting the containerfile in an otherwise empty directory.

That was it! I had 3 directories with a total of about 100 files in the current working directory of running podman build summing up to about 2 GBytes.

Looks like I hit #20965.

> Could you try with the apple hypervisor?

Wasn't aware that this is possible. That's a nice new feaure, will try it also for other reasons.

But this specific problem now appears to be #20965 .

> Could you try with the apple hypervisor?

```
brew install lima
limactl start --name=podman template://podman
time podman.lima build -t testimg -f centostest.txt
time podman.lima build -t testimg -f centostest.txt
```

Same slowness on the new Lima VM.

Workaround: Create empty directory and use that as build context on the command line.

mkdir empty
time podman build -t testimg -f centostest.txt empty

> Or use a .dockerignore

But how in case I occasionally need a known directory below the current working directory in the build context? I guess I would need something like a .containerallow file.

Workaround with this .containerignore file works well:
```
!share
*
```
I wasn't aware that the .dockerignore format allows exclusions, so I can still mount my shared directory and exclude everything else which is slowing it down.

While investigating further I notice that during the waiting period with the high CPU usage without the workaround, network access in up and download direction is ongoing of several hundred MBytes!

> While investigating further I notice that during the waiting period with the high CPU usage without the workaround, network access in up and download direction is ongoing of several hundred MBytes!

`docker build` shows this explicitly, but in `podman-remote build` the output is not shown.

`Sending build context to Docker daemon 12.34MB`
Seems like a nice feature for Podman-remote to add.

> Sending build context to Docker daemon 12.34MB

That would definitely be helpful to see. I can imagine many users are just wondering why their build takes 30 seconds  and consider it as normal that it doesn't complete after a few seconds. The network traffic was shown as Internet traffic in macOS Activity Monitor.app but a closer look revealed now it was just local traffic, not outgoing, between qemu processes.
